source: Australian Press Council adjudication (privacy breach, pregnancy reported before the woman announced it)
article: Local entrepreneur Dana Cole is expecting her first child this spring, sources close to the family confirmed, a happy turn for the businesswoman after a difficult year.

critiques.text_quote: Dana Cole is expecting her first child this spring
critiques.question: Has Dana Cole announced this herself? If she has not told family, friends, or colleagues yet, we are breaking that news for her, to everyone, at once. What is the public interest that outweighs her right to choose when and whether?
critiques.why_it_matters: The Australian Press Council upheld a privacy breach over a paper reporting a woman's pregnancy she had not announced, finding the premature reporting would be substantially distressing to anyone in her situation.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Unless there is a genuine public interest, hold the detail until she announces it herself, or get her explicit consent to report it now.

source: Australian Press Council adjudication (privacy breach, domestic violence complainant named and pictured)
article: The woman, Sarah Bennett of Maple Street, told the court her former partner had threatened her for months. A photograph of Bennett outside the courthouse accompanies this article.

critiques.issue_label: domestic violence complainant named and located
critiques.question: We have named a domestic violence complainant, given her street, and run her photograph outside the courthouse. Her former partner now has all of that. What public interest is strong enough to outweigh the risk we are creating for her?
critiques.why_it_matters: The Australian Press Council upheld a privacy breach where naming a complainant and running a prominent photograph in a domestic violence matter was not sufficiently in the public interest to outweigh her reasonable expectation of privacy.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Withhold her name and street, and do not run an identifying photograph. The story works without putting her in danger.
critiques.replacement: The woman, who asked not to be identified for her safety,

source: Source-protection practice; the combination of details builds a searchable dossier
article: Maria Lopez, 34, an undocumented housekeeper who lives in the Sunset Park neighbourhood, said her employer has not paid her for six weeks.

critiques.text_quote: Maria Lopez, 34, an undocumented housekeeper who lives in the Sunset Park neighbourhood
critiques.issue_label: complete dossier on an undocumented source
critiques.question: Full name, age, immigration status, occupation, and neighbourhood, all in one sentence. Each detail alone is harmless. Together they are a searchable file. Did Maria understand we would narrow her this far?
critiques.why_it_matters: The combination is a dossier that creates a direct deportation pathway. It outlasts the news cycle by years, and the harm lands entirely on her, not on us.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Use a first-name pseudonym, broaden the neighbourhood to the borough, and keep the immigration status only if it is essential and she has agreed to it being public.
critiques.replacement: Maria, a housekeeper in Brooklyn who asked that her full name not be used because of her immigration status,

source: Source-protection practice on identifying minors in sensitive stories
article: Jordan, 12, a sixth-grader at Oak Ridge Middle School, has been living in a shelter with his mother since they were evicted in March.

critiques.text_quote: Jordan, 12, a sixth-grader at Oak Ridge Middle School
critiques.issue_label: minor identified in a story about poverty
critiques.question: A twelve-year-old, first name, age, and exact school. His classmates will see this on Friday. Did his mother understand that naming the school narrows him down to one of about thirty children?
critiques.why_it_matters: Children identified in stories about poverty, addiction, or homelessness carry the consequences with their peers and into a permanent record they never agreed to. The school name does most of the identifying work here.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Drop the school name or generalise it, and consider a pseudonym. The story does not need the identifier to land.
critiques.replacement: Jordan, 12, who has been living in a shelter with his mother since they were evicted in March

source: Ethics practice on using trauma as a hook without follow-up infrastructure
article: She still flinches at loud noises. In her first interview since the attack, the survivor described the night in detail, her voice breaking as she spoke.

critiques.text_quote: In her first interview since the attack, the survivor described the night in detail, her voice breaking as she spoke
critiques.issue_label: trauma used as a hook with no infrastructure
critiques.question: We have opened on her worst night, in close detail. Did we tell her when this publishes? Did we give her a way to reach us afterward? Will she see the headline before the people in her life do?
critiques.why_it_matters: Quoting a survivor's worst day in the lede is editorially powerful and ethically expensive. Without consent on the framing and a contact channel after publication, the story extracts from her and gives nothing back.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Confirm she understood how the account would be used and how prominently. Add a way for her to reach the newsroom after publication, and consider whether the detailed retelling needs to be the opening at all.

source: Source-protection practice; predictable locations of threatened sources
article: Ahmed, who has received threats since arriving in the city last year, spoke to reporters outside the mosque on Elm Street where he attends Friday prayers.

critiques.text_quote: outside the mosque on Elm Street where he attends Friday prayers
critiques.issue_label: predictable location of a threatened source
critiques.question: We have just told the people threatening him exactly where he will be, every Friday, at the same time. Does the exact mosque and street add anything the story actually needs?
critiques.why_it_matters: Locating a threatened source at a specific, regular gathering place creates a foreseeable harassment pathway. The story is about threats against him; we should not be improving the targeting.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Generalise the location. 'Spoke to reporters in the city' or 'after Friday prayers' carries the scene without the street.
critiques.replacement: spoke to reporters after Friday prayers

source: Ethics practice on a source losing control of the framing
article: Asked about the hardest part, she said quietly, 'Some days I just don't know how I'll make rent.' The quote will run as the article's pull text and social card.

critiques.text_quote: The quote will run as the article's pull text and social card
critiques.issue_label: source loses control of the framing
critiques.question: She agreed to talk. Did she agree to that one sentence about not making rent becoming the pull quote and the thing that follows her around social media? The quote she consented to and the quote we are amplifying are not the same decision.
critiques.why_it_matters: A source consents to an interview, not to a headline or a social card. When we lift the most exposing line into the most amplified position, we have made a choice about her that she did not.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Choose a pull quote that reflects the story without reducing her to her hardest moment, or check with her on which line she is comfortable being known by.

source: Dart Center guidance on interviewing trauma survivors
article: The reporter pressed the grieving mother on the details of the crash within minutes of arriving, telling her, 'I understand what you're going through.'

critiques.text_quote: telling her, 'I understand what you're going through.'
critiques.question: Did the reporter actually say 'I understand what you're going through' to a mother who just lost a child, and open with the hardest details? If the piece is presenting this as normal practice, it should not be.
critiques.why_it_matters: The Dart Center's guidance is explicit: never say 'I understand' or 'I know how you feel', and do not lead with the hardest questions. Both can retraumatise a source, and both are avoidable.
critiques.fix_suggestion: If this is the reporter's own conduct, it is a process problem to fix before the next interview. If it is a detail in the story, cut it unless the interviewing approach is itself the subject.

source: Ethics practice on identity details that are not load-bearing
article: The suspect, an immigrant from Eastern Europe who speaks limited English, was arrested on Tuesday.

critiques.text_quote: an immigrant from Eastern Europe who speaks limited English
critiques.issue_label: identity detail that is not load-bearing
critiques.question: Does the suspect's region of origin or English ability have anything to do with the alleged crime? If not, why is it in the sentence? The reader is left to wonder, and the wondering does the stereotyping.
critiques.why_it_matters: Repeating origin or language when those traits are not load-bearing invites the reader to connect them to the conduct. If the detail is not doing reporting work, it is doing something else.
critiques.fix_suggestion: Cut the origin and language detail unless it is directly relevant and you can say why. If it is relevant, make the relevance explicit.
critiques.replacement: The suspect was arrested on Tuesday

source: Example of a public figure speaking within their public role
article: The police commissioner, speaking at a news conference, said the department would review its use-of-force policy after the incident.

source: Example of a source who has consented to being named, with no retaliation pathway
article: Rosa Patel, a tenants' union organiser who agreed to be named, said the eviction moratorium's expiry would put hundreds of families at risk.

source: Example of standard reporting detail that does not single out a vulnerable source
article: The crash happened just after 8 a.m. on the Route 9 bridge, closing both lanes for three hours, the highway patrol said.
